analogy: Think of "Water Oceans" through the chapter's own opening images: MOVEMENTS OF OCEAN WATER T he ocean water is dynamic. Its physical characteristics like temperature, salinity, density and the external forces like of the sun, moon and the winds influence the movement of ocean water. The horizontal and vertical motions are common in ocean water... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Water Oceans is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that mOVEMENTS OF OCEAN WATER T he ocean water is dynamic.

The text explains that its physical characteristics like temperature, salinity, density and the external forces like of the sun, moon and the winds influence the movement of ocean water.

The text explains that the horizontal and vertical motions are common in ocean water bodies.

The text explains that the horizontal motion refers to the ocean currents and waves.

The text explains that ocean currents are the continuous flow of huge amount of water in a definite direction while the waves are the horizontal motion of water.

The text explains that water moves ahead from one place to another through ocean currents while the water in the waves does not move, but the wave trains move ahead.

The text explains that the vertical motion refers to the rise and fall of water in the oceans and seas.

The text explains that due to attraction of the sun and the moon, the ocean water is raised up and falls down twice a day.

The text explains that the upwelling of cold water from subsurface and the sinking of surface water are also forms of vertical motion of ocean water.

The text explains that wAVES Waves are actually the energy, not the water as such, which moves across the ocean surface.

The text explains that water particles only travel in a small circle as a wave passes.

The text explains that wind causes waves to travel in the ocean and the energy is released on shorelines.

The text explains that the motion of the surface water seldom affects the stagnant deep bottom water of the oceans.

The text explains that as a wave approaches the beach, it slows down.

The text explains that this is due to the friction occurring between the dynamic water and the sea floor.

The text explains that and, when the depth of water is less than half the wavelength of the wave, the wave breaks.

The text explains that the largest waves are found in the open oceans.

The text explains that waves continue to grow larger as they move and absorb energy from the wind.

The text explains that most of the waves are caused by the wind driving against water.

The text explains that when a breeze of two knots or less blows over calm water, small ripples form and grow as the wind speed increases until white caps appear in the breaking waves.

The text explains that waves may travel thousands of km before rolling ashore, breaking and dissolving as surf.

The text explains that steep waves are fairly young ones and are probably formed by local wind.

The text explains that slow and steady waves originate from far away places, possibly from another hemisphere.

The text explains that the maximum wave height is determined by the strength of the wind, i.e. how long it blows and the area over which it blows in a single direction.

The text explains that waves travel because wind pushes the water body in its course while gravity pulls the crests of the waves downward.

The text explains that the falling water pushes the former troughs upward, and the Figure13.1 : Motion of waves and water molecules C H A P T E R

The text explains that mOVEMENTS OF OCEAN WATER 109 wave moves to a new position (Figure 13.1).

The text explains that the actual motion of the water beneath the waves is circular.

The text explains that it indicates that things are carried up and forward as the wave approaches, and down and back as it passes.

The text explains that characteristics of Waves Wave crest and trough : The highest and lowest points of a wave are called the crest and trough respectively.

The text explains that wave height : It is the vertical distance from the bottom of a trough to the top of a crest of a wave.

The text explains that wave amplitude : It is one-half of the wave height.

The text explains that wave period : It is merely the time interval between two successive wave crests or troughs as they pass a fixed point.

The text explains that wavelength : It is the horizontal distance between two successive crests.

The text explains that wave speed : It is the rate at which the wave moves through the water, and is measured in knots.

The text explains that wave frequency : It is the number of waves passing a given point during a one- second time interval.

The text explains that movement of water caused by meteorological effects (winds and atmospheric pressure changes) are called surges.

The text explains that types of Tides Tides vary in their frequency, direction and movement from place to place and also from time to time.

The text explains that spring tides : The position of both the sun and the moon in relation to the earth has direct bearing on tide height.

The text explains that they represent a regular volume of water in a definite path and direction.

The text explains that differences in water density affect vertical mobility of ocean currents.

The text explains that these waters move around the ocean basins due to variations in the density and gravity.

The text explains that deep waters sink into the deep ocean basins at high latitudes, where the temperatures are cold enough to cause the density to increase.

The text explains that the mixing of warm and cold currents help to replenish the oxygen and favour the growth of planktons, the primary food for fish population.

The text explains that the best fishing grounds of the world exist mainly in these mixing zones.

The text explains that (i) Upward and downward movement of ocean water is known as the : (a) tide (c) wave (b) current (d) none of the above (ii) Spring tides are caused : (a) As result of the moon and the sun pulling the earth gravitationally in the same direction.

The text explains that (b) As result of the moon and the sun pulling the earth gravitationally in the opposite direction.

The text explains that (iii) The distance between the earth and the moon is minimum when the moon is in : (a) Aphelion (c) Perihelion (b) Perigee (d) Apogee (iv) The earth reaches its perihelion in: (a) October (c) July (b) September (d) January 2.

The text explains that (ii) Where do waves in the ocean get their energy from?

The text explains that how does it affect the temperature of coastal areas in the N.

The text explains that throw a stone and notice how waves are generated.

The text explains that (ii) Take a globe and a map showing the currents of the oceans.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 13: Water Oceans

Water moves ahead from one place to another through ocean currents while the water in the waves does not move, but the wave trains move ahead. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 1)
The vertical motion refers to the rise and fall of water in the oceans and seas. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 1)
Due to attraction of the sun and the moon, the ocean water is raised up and falls down twice a day. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 1)
The upwelling of cold water from subsurface and the sinking of surface water are also forms of vertical motion of ocean water. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 1)

cases.name: Characteristics
cases.point: Characteristics of Waves Wave crest and trough : The highest and lowest points of a wave are called the crest and trough respectively. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 1)

cases.name: FUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY
cases.point: FUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 110 Tides of Bay of Fundy, Canada The highest tides in the world occur in the Bay of Fundy in Nova Scotia,... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 2)

cases.name: Types
cases.point: Types of Tides Tides vary in their frequency, direction and movement from place to place and also from time to time. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 2)

cases.name: Tides
cases.point: Tides based on Frequency Semi-diurnal tide : The most common tidal pattern, featuring two high tides and two low tides each day. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 2)

cases.name: These
cases.point: These tides generally occur along the west coast of North America and on many islands of the Pacific Ocean. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch13 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat MOVEMENTS OF OCEAN WATER and WAVES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Water Oceans; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Water Oceans through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use MOVEMENTS OF OCEAN WATER and WAVES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
